summary: I spent almost my entire childhood in Catskill, New York, where I was born and raised. I went through elementary, middle, and high school there before heading off to college. My dad's Army Reserve detachment was also located in Catskill, so I have memories of visiting the big armory there as a kid.

My family briefly lived in Washington state for about half a year during my childhood, but my mom couldn't stand the weather so we moved back to Catskill, New York. It was a short interlude in an otherwise Catskill-centered upbringing.

entity: State University of New York at Oswego
summary: I went away to college at the State University of New York at Oswego, where I graduated with a mathematics degree in education. I paid my own way through school since my family couldn't afford it. After graduating, I fulfilled my dream of becoming a math teacher.

My dad served during World War II as a Navy gunner on the back of a PBY Catalina in the Pacific theater. After the war ended, he transferred into the Army Reserve and served for 30 years. His military service during WWII was a significant part of his life and influenced my early experiences.

After World War II, my dad joined the United States Army Reserve as a warrant officer and served for 30 years. One of his detachments was located in Catskill, New York, where I remember visiting the armory as a kid. Later, he moved to Buffalo, New York, where he continued his reserve duties.

My dad was a Navy gunner on the back of a PBY Catalina during World War II, serving in the Pacific theater. That aircraft was part of his wartime experience before he transitioned to the Army Reserve. It was a key part of his military story that I grew up hearing about.

After my parents divorced, my dad moved to Buffalo, New York, where he continued his Army Reserve work. We visited him there sometimes, and that city became an important place in his later military career. It was different from my childhood home in Catskill but still connected through family.

As a kid, I was involved in the Boy Scouts of America and spent a lot of time there. It was a big part of my childhood, and I eventually earned the rank of Eagle Scout. That experience was one of my early connections to disciplined, structured activities.

After teaching high school for two years, I was drafted and chose to join the United States Air Force instead of the Army. I entered as an officer candidate because of my college degree and went through officer training at Lackland Air Force Base. I trained as a navigator and specialized in electronic warfare, eventually serving in combat roles.

My older brother enlisted in the Air Force in the early 1960s and went through training in Mississippi. He became a code breaker and served overseas in Europe and Africa. His military service was part of my family's connection to the armed forces.

When I enlisted in the Air Force, I was flown from Syracuse to San Antonio, Texas, which was an exciting experience for me. From there, I was taken to Lackland Air Force Base to begin my officer training. That trip marked the start of my military career.

I was living in Syracuse, New York, when I enlisted in the Air Force and was flown from there to San Antonio for training. Syracuse was my home before entering the military, and I remember that first airplane ride vividly. It was a big change from my teaching job in New York.

I went through my initial Air Force officer training at Lackland Air Force Base after arriving in San Antonio. It was a strange and disciplined environment, with things like getting our heads shaved and being organized into flights. That three-month training prepared me to become a second lieutenant.

After officer training, I was sent to Mayther Air Force Base for about eleven months of navigator training. That was where I earned my navigator qualifications and learned about my future role in electronic warfare. It was an important phase before specialized training.

During my electronic warfare training, I learned that most bombardiers went into the B-52 Stratofortress. However, I was in a different track focused on electronic warfare, so I didn't fly on the B-52. It was interesting to see how different aircraft had specialized crews and roles.

One of the aircraft options for electronic warfare officers was the General Dynamics F-111 Aardvark, where the back seater acted as the electronic warfare officer. It was similar to being a fighter pilot but with specialized duties. I knew people who trained for that assignment while I was in training.

A friend of mine got a reconnaissance slot flying the RB-57 Canberra, a high altitude reconnaissance plane. It was a specialized assignment within the electronic warfare and reconnaissance community. I remember that as an example of the variety of aircraft we trained for.

I specialized in electronic warfare after navigator training and was one of only seven selected for that slot. The training was very specialized, and I went through additional schools including survival training in Washington and Florida. I flew on the EB-66 Destroyer, monitoring and jamming enemy radar and communications during combat missions.

Part of my survival training for electronic warfare took place at Homestead Air Reserve Base in south Florida. This was one of the last steps before going into operational assignments. The training prepared me for the challenges of combat flying.

I trained at Shaw Air Force Base for electronic warfare on the EB-66 aircraft. Since all the planes were overseas, we did simulator training to learn the systems. Shaw was where I learned to monitor and jam enemy signals before deploying to combat zones.

I flew the EB-66 Destroyer in electronic warfare roles, first in the reconnaissance version monitoring enemy signals, then as a jamming platform disrupting communications and radar. The plane had a crew of three on the jamming model, and I had increased responsibilities including co-pilot duties and lookout for threats. It was a critical part of my combat experience.

entity: Korat Royal Thai Air Force Base
summary: I was stationed at Korat Royal Thai Air Force Base in Thailand during my combat deployment. That’s where I flew my first combat missions on the EB-66. I also formed close friendships there, including with Randy Jones, who was my fellow electronic warfare officer.

I went to Ton Son Nhut Air Base with my friend Yavon Clements, who was in a class ahead of me. It was part of our deployment in Southeast Asia during the Vietnam War. Ton Son Nhut was a key location for air operations in the region.

Before flying combat missions, I got a 'dollar ride' in a KC-135 Stratotanker where we practiced aerial refueling of different airplanes. It was a way to keep us current and maintain combat pay, even though we weren't yet flying operational missions. That experience was exciting and helped prepare us.

During my time flying the EB-66, we flew missions over Laos, especially along the border where there was a lot of anti-aircraft fire but no surface-to-air missiles. We would monitor enemy signals and sometimes fly into Northern Laos, which was dangerous but part of our operational area before entering North Vietnam.

My combat service took place during the Vietnam War, flying electronic warfare missions from bases like Korat and Ton Son Nhut. We flew dangerous missions into North Vietnam and surrounding areas, jamming enemy radar and communications. It was a challenging time that tested all our training and skills.

Although not explicitly mentioned, my combat missions and electronic warfare role coincided with operations like Linebacker II during the Vietnam War. The intense bombing campaigns required us to jam enemy radar and protect strike aircraft. My training and missions were part of that larger conflict effort.

Guam was mentioned as one of the places where the KC-135 Stratotankers operated during refueling missions. While I didn’t fly directly from Guam, it was part of the broader network supporting air operations in Southeast Asia. It was one of the key logistical hubs in the region.
